peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarian anti-domination and moderate participatory democracy leanings from the GAL-TAN model provide mild support, but low openness to experience and authoritarian submission dampen enthusiasm.

Low agreeableness and high conscientiousness with a strong preference for order and competence make this person cautiously supportive of citizen assemblies as a structured, deliberative input, but their low trust in institutions and moderate populist attitudes temper enthusiasm.
Moderate support due to high agreeableness and populist people-centrism, but tempered by very low openness to experience and a preference for strong leadership.

Despite high openness, very low GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (17.35) and strong authoritarianism (85.32) make this person skeptical of citizen-led deliberation, seeing it as inefficient and threatening to established order.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (74.85) and strong anti-domination moral foundation (74.77) drive moderate support, but low openness to experience (11.11) and very low internal political efficacy (16.72) temper enthusiasm for novel deliberative processes.
High agreeableness and moderate openness support participatory democracy, but very low conscientiousness and low system justification temper enthusiasm for a process requiring sustained civic effort.

Very low openness and low agreeableness, combined with high authoritarianism and social dominance orientation, make this person skeptical of participatory democratic innovations that could challenge established hierarchies.
High system justification and low political trust lead me to see citizen assemblies as a threat to established order and elite competence, so I lean against them.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Conscientiousness and dutifulness align with rule-following norms, but very low agreeableness and high collective narcissism suggest conditional acceptance rather than strong idealism.

High conscientiousness and a strong sense of dutifulness, combined with very low right-wing authoritarianism and a postconventional moral stage, support a firm commitment to democratic norms like loser consent, though low political efficacy slightly reduces maximal endorsement.
Clear support driven by high agreeableness, fairness concerns, and a secure attachment style, though collective narcissism and a competitive worldview create some ambivalence.

High system justification (82.21) and authoritarian law-and-order preference (90.95) support accepting electoral outcomes as part of maintaining social stability, though low external efficacy (3.23) tempers enthusiasm.

Very high socio-cultural liberalism (91.2) and strong pluralism/diversity support (98.47) produce clear support, though moderate dangerous-worldview (73.68) and high collective narcissism (80.77) introduce some reservations about accepting outcomes from out-group winners.
High agreeableness and strong fairness concerns (low fairness foundation) are offset by high RWA submission and collective narcissism, producing moderate support for this democratic norm.

High civic nationalism and moderate system justification, alongside a conventional moral stage, support accepting electoral outcomes as a procedural norm, though low trust in others tempers enthusiasm.
My high fairness/equality moral foundation and civic nationalism value democratic processes, but my low trust in institutions and high authoritarian submission temper this into mild support.
